---
title: "Planning as a Control Layer"
chapter_id: "planning-as-a-control-layer"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "planforge"
  - "viea"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Planning as a Control Layer

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `planning-as-a-control-layer` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `planforge`, `viea`, `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `planforge`, `viea`; supporting: `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `planforge`, `viea`, `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `moecot`; raw cache: `planforge`, `viea`, `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Goals need to become governed plans with dependencies, budgets, risk limits, tool choices, and stopping conditions.

Planning begins after the command contract is explicit. Its job is to decide what work should exist, in what order, with what context, tools, budgets, authority, risk controls, and verification. It should not own memory, perform side effects, or decide that safety constraints are inconvenient. PlanForge supplies the planning middleware frame; Cognitive Compilation supplies the compiler pipeline frame; VIEA supplies the execution spine that the plan must feed.

The planner is therefore a control layer, not a general cognition blob. It turns contracts into dispatchable structure while preserving constraints and leaving execution authority to downstream gates.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Planning cannot be collapsed into memory, reasoning, or execution because each layer has different authority and failure modes.

A memory layer retrieves context; it should not silently create work. A reasoning layer evaluates claims; it should not silently spend tool authority. An execution layer runs jobs; it should not be the first place dependencies and stop conditions are discovered. When these roles collapse, scope creep looks like initiative, tool overreach looks like competence, and replanning after failure becomes ad hoc.

Planning also needs to be separate from final answers because plans are cheap to reject. A bad plan can be repaired before side effects occur. A bad execution may need rollback, audit, or incident handling.

## Core Claim

[planning-as-a-control-layer.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Planning is a separate control layer that converts accepted goals into governed action without owning memory, reasoning, or side effects.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - PlanForge, Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture, Cognitive Compilation, Software Magic Grimoire, and MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Plan Graph.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Command contract"] --> B["Assumption extraction"]
  B --> C["Task graph"]
  C --> D["Dependency + context requests"]
  D --> E["Authority / risk / budget annotation"]
  E --> F["Verification plan"]
  F --> G{"Dispatchable?"}
  G -- "yes" --> H["Typed jobs"]
  G -- "no" --> I["Plan residual / replan"]
  H --> J["Runtime feedback"]
  J --> K{"Replan needed?"}
  K -- "yes" --> C
  K -- "no" --> L["Plan ledger"]
  I --> L
```

- Compile goals into strategic, tactical, and runtime plans.
- Represent assumptions, task graph, dependency graph, required context, tools, worker requirements, authority requirements, budgets, verification plan, replanning policy, stopping conditions, and failure behavior.
- Delegate execution through typed contracts.

Strategic planning chooses the broad route. Tactical planning decomposes work and dependencies. Runtime planning creates dispatchable nodes and replanning policies. The boundaries matter because only the runtime layer should request specific tools, adapters, or human approvals.

The planner should be allowed to say "I do not have enough context," "this dependency is cyclic," "this tool exceeds authority," or "this budget cannot satisfy the quality predicate." Those outputs are useful plan residuals, not failures to be hidden.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Plan Graph.

- Alignment filters goals.
- VCM supplies context packets.
- Routing selects specialists.
- Execution runs typed jobs.

Minimum fields:

- `plan_id`
- `command_contract`
- `assumptions`
- `nodes`
- `dependencies`
- `context_requests`
- `tool_requirements`
- `authority_requirements`
- `risk_budget`
- `compute_budget`
- `verification_plan`
- `replanning_policy`
- `stop_conditions`
- `dispatch_state`

Alignment filters goals before planning. VCM supplies context packets. Routing consumes capability requirements. Execution receives typed jobs. Evidence records the plan's assumptions, residuals, verification results, and replanning events.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Plans expose constraints and stop conditions.
- Runtime replanning preserves authority limits.
- Tool selection is justified by task requirements.

A plan node inherits the parent command's authority ceiling unless governance narrows or explicitly changes it. Runtime replanning may change order, route, or decomposition, but it cannot broaden the authority boundary by calling itself adaptation.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Scope creep.
- Planning without replanning.
- Tool choice exceeds authority or budget.

Scope creep occurs when a plan adds goals that were never accepted. Planning without replanning means failures create improvisation instead of controlled repair. Tool choice exceeding authority or budget means the plan is not dispatchable, even if the work would be useful.

## Minimal implementation

A plan graph format with assumptions, task graph, dependencies, context and tool requirements, authority requirements, budgets, verification plan, replanning policy, stop conditions, and failure behavior.

The smallest useful implementation is a `plan_graph` schema and fixture. It validates that a plan has nodes, dependencies, context and tool requirements, authority requirements, budgets, verification, replanning, stop conditions, and dispatch state. It does not prove the plan is good.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Decomposition accuracy test | Check whether plan nodes preserve the accepted command objective and constraints. | planned; not run |
| Dependency ordering test | Check whether dependencies precede dependents before dispatch. | planned; not run |
| Context-demand prediction test | Check whether each node declares needed context before execution. | planned; not run |
| Runtime replanning test | Check whether replanning preserves authority, stop conditions, and residual history. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:planning.control_layer.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Planning` | A plan node inherits the authority ceiling of its parent contract unless governance lowers it. | planned |
| `lean:planning.control_layer.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Planning` | A plan with unsatisfied required constraints cannot be dispatched. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `planforge` | PlanForge | planning_control | Planning substrate. Goal-to-execution compilation, hierarchical decomposition, DAG planning, scheduling, intelligence arbitrage. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Planning as a Control Layer converts accepted commands into governed structure. It exposes assumptions, tasks, dependencies, context requests, tools, authority, risk, budgets, verification, stop conditions, and replanning policy before execution begins.

This layer makes action governable by making non-action explicit: missing context, blocked authority, cyclic dependencies, exhausted budget, and failed predicates all become plan residuals instead of hidden improvisation.
